steps.title: Identify the grammar issue
steps.content: The blank falls between two independent clauses: "...evidence that it was occupied [blank]" and "In 1420 CE, according to Burger, humans were likely inhabiting the area." Two independent clauses cannot be joined by a comma alone (comma splice) or run together without any punctuation (fused sentence). A period is the correct separator.

steps.title: Eliminate wrong choices, confirm the answer
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘earlier. In’: Places a period between the two independent clauses, correctly creating two separate sentences. ✓
  • Choice B — ‘earlier, in’: Uses only a comma to join two independent clauses — a comma splice, which violates the rule against joining independent clauses with a comma alone. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘earlier, which in’: Also a comma splice; additionally, the subordinating conjunction "which" makes the second clause modify the first in a confusing, illogical way. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘earlier in’: Fuses two independent clauses with no punctuation at all — a run-on sentence. ✗
That confirms A is correct.
